Reference Object  ·  Catalogue No. RO-BRONZE-037  ·  Bronze / Archaeometallurgy

Bronze Ritual Vessel with Lid, Fu-type (簠)

Rectangular archaic food vessel with fitted lid, dragon-head loop handles, and openwork ring-scroll uprights

Attribution: Late Zhou dynasty, ca. 6th–5th century BCE
Proposed origin: Eastern Henan, Luo River basin
Material: archaic Cu–Sn–Sb–As(–Ni) bronze, no deliberate bulk lead
Fig. 1 — Full view: fitted lid and body, dragon-head loop handles, openwork ring-scroll uprights, taotie/interlace bands.
Fig. 2 — Dragon-head loop handle, detail.
Fig. 3 — Interior, open: raised central boss within scroll-ornament panel.
Fig. 4 — Shoulder and handle uprights, detail.
Fig. 5 — Interior base, showing malachite-green patina and encrustation.

01 Object Description and Attribution

The vessel is a rectangular bronze food container of fitted-lid construction (fu-type, 簠), with gently rounded walls, a squared base with smoothed corners, and four short feet. Dragon-head loop handles are set at the body sides (Fig. 2), and openwork ring-and-scroll uprights rise from the lid and body rim (Figs. 1, 4). The body and lid carry taotie-derived and interlace ornament bands (Figs. 1, 4); the interior, visible with the lid open, shows a raised central boss within a scroll-ornament panel (Fig. 3). The interior base retains a heavy malachite-green archaeological patina with encrustation (Fig. 5), consistent with prolonged natural burial.

The vessel is attributed to the late Zhou dynasty, ca. 6th–5th century BCE, with proposed origin in eastern Henan, Luo River basin, on the combined basis of form, wall cross-section, and the alloy and patina evidence set out below, which correspond closely to published comparanda from the Henan Provincial and Baoji museum collections.

02 SEM–EDX Diagnostic Analysis

The metallurgical dataset combines four analytical campaigns carried out by the author: point analysis of metal shavings from the vessel rim, point analysis of the lid interior patina, analysis of loose mineral powder from internal recesses, and a supplementary three-point matrix and two-point patina survey with a compositional linescan, from a separate sampling occasion.

2.1 Metallic matrix — rim shavings (4-point survey)

ElementRange recorded (%)Note
Cu76–80principal alloy metal
Sn7–10tin-bronze alloying component
Sb~1.2–1.6archaic ore-derived trace element
As1.6–2.2archaic ore-derived trace element
Pblocalised up to 94.7 (isolated points)casting residue / secondary leaching zone, not bulk alloy
C2–4.6organic / soil contamination
Oup to 3.6 (surface)surface oxidation

The matrix is a Cu–Sn alloy with consistent antimony and arsenic co-occurrence, a recognised signature of pre-industrial, unrefined-ore pyrometallurgy. The isolated 94.7% lead reading is a highly localised point value, consistent with a lead inclusion or globule — a well-documented feature of leaded and semi-leaded archaic bronzes — rather than a bulk alloy constituent.

2.2 Lid interior patina (4-point survey)

ElementRange recorded (%)
O23.2–88.9
C14.0–61.4
Cl0.2–5.1
Pb0.4–2.4
Cu0.5–9.7
Si, Al, Cavariable, present

The patina shows a well-developed microporous crust with fractal-pattern breakdown zones and fibrous/crystalline aggregates, consistent with prolonged soil interaction rather than an applied coating. High oxygen with silica/aluminosilicate phases points to infiltration of clay- or loam-type burial soil; chlorine and lead levels are consistent with long residence in a humid environment permitting local ionic migration.

2.3 Internal powder residue (3-point survey)

ComponentConcentration range
SiO₂ (Si / O)Si up to 21.9%, O up to 78.1%
CuO (Cu)up to 58.9%
Trace: C, Al, Fe, K, Cl, Caminor, present

Secondary mineral formation from corrosion and desiccation within the vessel’s internal recesses — soil particulate fused with copper oxidation products.

2.4 Supplementary matrix survey (cross-sectioned shaving, 3 points + linescan)

ElementSpot 2 (at.%)Spot 3 (at.%)Spot 4 (at.%)
Cu48.849.949.9
Pb2.8—0.6
O11.98.33.4
Sb1.52.44.4
Sn1.81.92.3
As1.33.32.7
Ni——0.9
C31.934.18.2

Spot 4’s nickel reading (0.9%) points to a complex sulphide-bearing copper ore source, plausibly in the Sanmenxia–Luoyang region, consistent with the vessel’s proposed findspot. The compositional linescan (As 2.6%, Pb 0.3%, Ni 0.9%) confirms the heterogeneous distribution of trace impurities characteristic of cast archaic bronze.

2.5 Supplementary surface patina survey

ZoneComposition (at.%)Interpretation
Patina 1 — carbonate–chlorideC 61.4, O 23.2, Cu 9.7, Cl 5.1copper cuprochloride and humic patina, consistent with archaeological burial
Patina 2 — aluminosilicate inclusionO 53.1, Si 14.1, Al 11.9, C 14.0, K/Mg/Fe tracessoil formation from clay particles embedded in surface microcracks
Alloy

Cu–Sn–Sb–As(–Ni) bronze, no deliberately introduced bulk lead across all matrix zones.

Patina

Multi-mineral, layered corrosion (cuprochlorides, humic phases, clay infiltration) consistent with natural burial.

Ore source

Ni presence indicates complex sulphide-bearing ore, plausibly Sanmenxia–Luoyang region.

Casting

Preserved primary casting microstructure, no evidence of modern reconstruction.

03 Comparative Analysis

ComparandumRegionNote
Fu vessel, Henan Provincial Museum (Luoyang)HenanCu–Sb–As bronze without deliberate Pb addition
Baoji Bronze MuseumShaanxinickel recorded as a natural ore impurity
Zhouyuan excavationShaanxiZhou-period cauldrons with As and Sb up to 4%

The alloy recipe (Cu–Sb–As, absence of deliberate Pb, presence of Ni) and the patina character together correspond closely to these comparanda from Henan and Shaanxi, supporting a late Zhou attribution and, more specifically, local production consistent with regional ore sources.

04 Conclusion

The bronze fu-type vessel is manufactured from an archaic Cu–Sn–Sb–As(–Ni) bronze alloy, without technologically introduced bulk lead. It demonstrates natural, multi-stage patina formation and soil-derived mineralisation fully consistent with prolonged archaeological burial, and corresponds typologically to late Zhou dynasty ritual vessels of the fu class. On the basis of composition, patina structure and morphology, the object is consistent with the archaeological and museum analogues cited above.
